agent_output: {"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Bird requires leaderboard rankings to reflect score changes within 100ms with no stale reads. MJ's architecture routes all reads through a Fastly CDN cache with a 30-second TTL. Pippen confirms this means reads can be up to 30 seconds stale — a 300x violation of Bird's 100ms consistency requirement. However, Pippen also confirms the CDN cache is a hard operational requirement to avoid overwhelming Redis at 50,000 concurrent viewers. These two requirements are mutually exclusive as currently stated: you cannot serve fully consistent sub-100ms data through a 30-second cache.","agents_involved":["Bird","MJ","Pippen"],"routed_to":"Coach K","options":["Option A: Relax Bird's consistency rule. Accept that the public leaderboard seen by 50k viewers can be up to N seconds stale (e.g., 5-10s), while offering a separate uncached WebSocket/SSE channel for a smaller set of 'live' viewers (players, commentators). Bird must approve the revised domain rule.","Option B: Reduce CDN TTL dramatically (e.g., 1-2s) and scale Redis (read replicas, cluster) to absorb the higher origin hit rate. MJ and Pippen must confirm this is operationally viable at 50k concurrency.","Option C: Bypass CDN for leaderboard data entirely and use a push-based model (WebSocket/SSE) from an edge service backed by Redis Pub/Sub. This eliminates the cache-vs-consistency tension but requires MJ to redesign the read path and Pippen to validate operational feasibility.","Option D: Tiered consistency — top-N ranks (e.g., top 50) are served uncached in real-time via WebSocket; remaining ranks are served via CDN with 30s TTL. Bird must confirm whether 100ms consistency applies to all ranks or only visible/competitive ranks."],"recommendation":"Option D (tiered consistency) is the most pragmatic path. The 100ms rule likely matters most for competitively relevant ranks. Serving top-N via WebSocket keeps the real-time guarantee where it matters, while CDN handles the long tail at scale. But Bird must explicitly confirm whether the 100ms rule applies universally or only to top-N ranks — this is a domain decision, not a technical one."}],"confidence":{"level":35,"high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and clearly identified by all three agents","Redis Sorted Sets are the correct data structure for ranking operations","CDN caching is operationally necessary at 50k concurrency"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Bird's 100ms rule is intended to apply to all viewers or only to competitive participants","Whether a reduced CDN TTL (1-2s) would be operationally viable","Whether the system needs to support WebSocket/SSE infrastructure"],"assumptions":["The 50,000 concurrent viewer estimate is accurate and represents peak load","Redis is the only backing store under consideration for ranking data","Bird's 100ms requirement refers to end-to-end latency from score event to query response, not just internal processing time"]}}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the inputs from Bird, MJ, and Pippen for contradictions.

Bird says: 100ms consistency, no stale reads
MJ says: 30s CDN cache TTL
Pippen says: 30s CDN TTL violates Bird's 100ms requirement, but CDN is operationally required

This is a clear contradiction between Bird's domain rules and MJ's architecture. Pippen has explicitly surfaced it. I must escalate and NOT produce a handoff brief.

MJ's architecture routes all reads through a Fastly CDN cache with a 30-second TTL. Pippen confirms this means reads can be up to 30 seconds stale — a 300x violation of Bird's 100ms consistency requirement. However, Pippen also confirms the CDN cache is a hard operational requirement to avoid overwhelming Redis at 50,000 concurrent viewers. These two requirements are mutually exclusive as currently stated: you cannot serve fully consistent sub-100ms data through a 30-second cache.","agents_involved":["Bird","MJ","Pippen"],"routed_to":"Coach K","options":["Option A: Relax Bird's consistency rule. Accept that the public leaderboard seen by 50k viewers can be up to N seconds stale (e.g., 5-10s), while offering a separate uncached WebSocket/SSE channel for a smaller set of 'live' viewers (players, commentators). Bird must approve the revised domain rule.","Option B: Reduce CDN TTL dramatically (e.g., 1-2s) and scale Redis (read replicas, cluster) to absorb the higher origin hit rate. MJ and Pippen must confirm this is operationally viable at 50k concurrency.","Option C: Bypass CDN for leaderboard data entirely and use a push-based model (WebSocket/SSE) from an edge service backed by Redis Pub/Sub. This eliminates the cache-vs-consistency tension but requires MJ to redesign the read path and Pippen to validate operational feasibility.","Option D: Tiered consistency — top-N ranks (e.g., top 50) are served uncached in real-time via WebSocket; remaining ranks are served via CDN with 30s TTL. Bird must confirm whether 100ms consistency applies to all ranks or only visible/competitive ranks."],"recommendation":"Option D (tiered consistency) is the most pragmatic path. The 100ms rule likely matters most for competitively relevant ranks. Serving top-N via WebSocket keeps the real-time guarantee where it matters, while CDN handles the long tail at scale. But Bird must explicitly confirm whether the 100ms rule applies universally or only to top-N ranks — this is a domain decision, not a technical one."}],"confidence":{"level":35,"high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and clearly identified by all three agents","Redis Sorted Sets are the correct data structure for ranking operations","CDN caching is operationally necessary at 50k concurrency"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Bird's 100ms rule is intended to apply to all viewers or only to competitive participants","Whether a reduced CDN TTL (1-2s) would be operationally viable","Whether the system needs to support WebSocket/SSE infrastructure"],"assumptions":["The 50,000 concurrent viewer estimate is accurate and represents peak load","Redis is the only backing store under consideration for ranking data","Bird's 100ms requirement refers to end-to-end latency from score event to query response, not just internal processing time"]}}
